Route builder — order, dates, book

Pick your start date, tick places and set nights for each — check-in and check-out dates are computed by chaining from the start date. Edit the # number to reorder: type 2 to slot a place into position 2, everything after shifts down and keeps its nights. The trip always ends back at Krakow. The trip map above redraws your route as you go.

#PlaceNightsCheck-inCheck-outKmHWeatherBook

Km are driving distances between consecutive stops; "~" legs are estimates. Hours assume ~95 km/h trip average incl. short breaks. Rows marked alternative are researched options near the route — hover for why, tick to swap them in. Weather is the stay's high/low from Open-Meteo; "≈" means last year's same dates (too far out for a forecast). Booking/Airbnb links open pre-filled searches — availability and prices are theirs.

The places — profiles

Every stop in one gallery. Places on your route in the builder above float to the top, numbered in visit order. Ticket links go to official operators.

Szczawnica

stop · PL

A historic spa town nestled between the Pieniny Mountains and the Dunajec River, offering dramatic gorge rafting, mineral springs, and scenic alpine hiking trails.

Stay: €60-110/n central spa area — cheaper guesthouses on the outskirts €40-70

Try: Oscypek (smoked sheep's cheese), Kwaśnica (sour cabbage soup), Grilled Dunajec trout, Józefina mineral water

  • Dunajec River Rafting (Spływ Dunajcem) ~€22 · Book in advance during July-August peak season to secure morning slots.
  • Pieniny National Park Peak Galleries (Trzy Korony & Sokolica) ~€2 · Tickets are required only to access the metal viewing platforms at the very top of the peaks.
  • Where to eat
  • Karczma u Polany — Traditional highlander tavern serving fresh grilled trout and hearty sour rye soup, ~€15-25/pp.
  • Restauracja Bohema — Elegant dining in the historic spa park featuring venison and local wild mushroom dishes, ~€25-40/pp.
  • Schronisko Orlica — Riverside mountain chalet serving authentic bryndza pierogi and local craft beers, ~€10-18/pp.
  • In & around
  • Homole Gorge — 10 min drive, a stunning limestone canyon in Jaworki with easy hiking trails
  • Niedzica Castle — 30 min drive, a medieval fortress overlooking Lake Czorsztyn with spooky legends
  • Červený Kláštor (Slovakia) — 25 min drive, a historic 14th-century monastery just across the border
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking near the Dunajec rafting terminal and main promenade fills up by 9 AM; carry cash for private parking lots.
  • Bring a reusable bottle to taste the healing, highly mineralized waters at the Pump Room in Dietl Square.
  • The Dunajec cycling path crosses into Slovakia; carry your ID/passport as you will cross the border seamlessly.
  • Interesting facts
  • Szczawnica owes its name to 'szczawy', the local highlander term for acidic mineral waters rich in carbon dioxide.
  • The traditional wooden rafts used on the Dunajec River have been operated by local highlanders (Flisacy) since the early 19th century.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Mikulov

stop · CZ

A stunning South Moravian wine town dominated by a clifftop castle, offering scenic hikes up Holy Hill and easy access to the UNESCO-listed Lednice-Valtice cultural landscape.

Stay: €70-120/n historic center — cheaper guesthouses (penzion) €50-80

Try: Pálava white wine, local Moravian goulash, pickled Hermelín cheese

  • Mikulov Castle (Zámek Mikulov) ~€10 · Guided tours of the historic wine cellars and the giant wine cask are highly recommended.
  • Dietrichstein Tomb ~€4 · Located right on the main square; check seasonal opening hours before visiting.
  • Where to eat
  • Bistro Drogerka — Trendy bistro on the main square serving modern Czech-international fusion and local wines, ~€15-25/pp.
  • Sojka & spol. — Cozy restaurant focusing on regional Moravian ingredients, homemade pasta, and local wines, ~€20-30/pp.
  • Kukanz — Excellent local wine bar and bistro with great tapas-style plates and local cheese boards, ~€15-25/pp.
  • In & around
  • Pálava Hills (Děvín) — 15 min drive, scenic limestone ridge hike with ruins of Děvičky Castle.
  • Lednice-Valtice Complex — 20 min drive, UNESCO-listed landscape with spectacular châteaux and English gardens.
  • Nové Mlýny Reservoirs — 15 min drive, large lakes offering windsurfing, yachting, and lakeside relaxation.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • A Czech highway vignette is required if you use the D2 motorway from Brno or Bratislava.
  • Parking in the historic center is heavily restricted; use the designated paid parking lots (P1 to P3) just outside the old town.
  • Many local wine cellars (sklepy) and smaller restaurants only accept cash (Czech Koruna), so keep some on hand.
  • Interesting facts
  • Mikulov Castle houses one of the largest historic wine casks in Europe, built in 1643 with a capacity of over 101,000 liters.
  • Holy Hill (Svatý Kopeček) is one of the oldest pilgrimage sites in Moravia, featuring a 17th-century chapel at its peak.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Hallstatt

stop · AT

Nestled between the sheer cliffs of the Dachstein Alps and a mirror-like lake, Hallstatt captivates with its postcard-perfect alpine architecture and 7,000 years of salt-mining history.

Stay: €180-300/n historic center — cheaper across the lake in Obertraun €100-150/n

Try: Reinanke (local lake whitefish), Wiener Schnitzel, Kaiserschmarrn, Hallstätter Bier

  • Salzwelten Hallstatt (Salt Mine & Funicular) ~€40 · Book online in advance to secure your funicular and mine tour time slot, especially in summer.
  • Hallstatt Charnel House (Beinhaus) ~€2 · Tickets are cash-only at the entrance of St. Michael's Chapel.
  • Where to eat
  • Restaurant Im Seehotel Grüner Baum — Freshly caught Hallstätter See trout served on a historic, romantic lakeside terrace, ~€35-50/pp.
  • Gasthof Zauner — Renowned for its wild game dishes and traditional Austrian lake fish preparations in a cozy, rustic setting, ~€30-45/pp.
  • In & around
  • Dachstein Krippenstein — 15 min drive, features the thrilling Five Fingers viewing platform and giant ice caves.
  • Gosausee — 20 min drive, a breathtaking alpine lake offering flat hiking trails and reflections of the Dachstein Glacier.
  • Bad Ischl — 25 min drive, a historic imperial spa town home to the Kaiservilla and famous Zauner pastry shop.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • The historic center is strictly car-free; overnight guests must park in designated external lots (P1/P2) and take the hotel shuttle.
  • Day-tripper crowds peak heavily between 10:00 and 16:00; plan your sightseeing early in the morning or late in the evening.
  • Ensure you purchase an Austrian motorway vignette before driving on the autobahn to avoid hefty fines.
  • Interesting facts
  • Hallstatt is home to the world's oldest salt mine, with active mining history dating back over 7,000 years.
  • The village is so iconic that a full-scale, mirror-image replica of it was constructed in Guangdong, China, in 2012.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Zell am See

stop · AT

A stunning alpine town where the crystal-clear Lake Zell meets the dramatic peaks of the Schmittenhöhe, offering a perfect mix of lakeside relaxation and high-altitude hiking.

Stay: €150-250/n lakeside or old town — cheaper in nearby Schüttdorf €90-140/n

Try: Zeller Reinanke (lake whitefish), Salzburger Nockerl, Kaspressknödel

  • Schmittenhöhe Cable Car Ticket ~€35-45 · Included in the Zell am See-Kaprun Summer Card if staying at partner accommodations.
  • Kaprun High Mountain Reservoirs ~€28 · Includes bus and open-air inclined lift access; check weather before booking.
  • Where to eat
  • Fischrestaurant Erlhof — Upscale lakeside dining specializing in freshly caught local lake trout and char (~€40-65/pp).
  • Restaurant Steinerwirt — Traditional Austrian tavern serving excellent Wiener Schnitzel and local venison dishes (~€25-40/pp).
  • Salzmannshütte — Mountain hut on Schmittenhöhe offering hearty Kaspressknödel soup and panoramic views (~€15-25/pp).
  • In & around
  • Grossglockner High Alpine Road — 30 min drive to toll gate, Austria's highest paved mountain pass road.
  • Sigmund Thun Gorge — 15 min drive, dramatic wooden boardwalks over rushing glacial waters.
  • Kitzsteinhorn Glacier — 20 min drive to valley station, year-round snow and panoramic viewing platform at 3,029m.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • An Austrian highway vignette is mandatory for the drive; buy it online or at gas stations before entering the country.
  • Many hotels provide the free Zell am See-Kaprun Summer Card, which covers local cable cars, buses, and lidos.
  • Parking in the town center is highly restricted and expensive; look for designated P1-P3 garages or park-and-ride lots.
  • Interesting facts
  • Lake Zell is filled with exceptionally pure drinking-water quality water, fed directly by alpine glaciers.
  • The town's landmark Grand Hotel, built in 1894 on a private peninsula, has hosted royalty and celebrities for over a century.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Litomyšl

stop · CZ

A UNESCO-listed Renaissance chateau adorned with exquisite sgraffito, coupled with a vibrant art scene and the birthplace of composer Bedřich Smetana, makes Litomyšl a cultural gem.

Stay: €70-120/n historic center — cheaper guesthouses on the outskirts €50-70

Try: Smetana cake (Smetanův dort), local freshwater trout, Pardubice gingerbread

  • Litomyšl Chateau ~€10-15 · Guided tours of the interiors are highly recommended; book in advance during the summer opera festival.
  • Portmoneum (Museum of Josef Váchal) ~€5 · A unique museum featuring intense, colorful murals by visionary graphic artist Josef Váchal.
  • Where to eat
  • Bohém (Hotel Aplaus) — Upscale dining featuring seasonal Czech ingredients, modern culinary twists, and excellent local wines — ~€25-40/pp
  • Restaurace Havelka — Traditional Czech cuisine with a modern touch, famous for its beef sirloin in cream sauce (svíčková) — ~€15-25/pp
  • In & around
  • Toulovcovy Maštale Nature Reserve — 25 min drive, pine forests and dramatic sandstone rock labyrinths perfect for hiking.
  • Nové Hrady Chateau — 20 min drive, a stunning Rococo castle often called the 'Czech Versailles' with beautiful French gardens.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • The historic main square (Smetanovo náměstí) has paid parking zones; look for ticket machines or use local parking apps.
  • If visiting in June or July, the Smetana's Litomyšl Opera Festival dominates the town, making accommodation and tickets extremely scarce.
  • Many smaller museums and local cafes may only accept cash (Czech Koruna - CZK), so keep some physical currency on hand.
  • Interesting facts
  • Litomyšl Chateau features over 8,000 unique sgraffito envelopes on its exterior walls, with no two designs being identical.
  • Famous Czech composer Bedřich Smetana was born in the chateau's brewery in 1824.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Karpacz

stop · PL

Nestled in the Karkonosze Mountains, Karpacz is a premier outdoor hub famous for its unique medieval Norwegian stave church, dramatic granite peaks, and scenic hiking trails leading to the summit of Śnieżka.

Stay: €60-110/n town center — cheaper guesthouses (pokoje gościnne) €40-70/n

Try: Karkonoski sour rye soup (Żurek), grilled oscypek cheese with cranberry, fresh mountain trout (Pstrąg)

  • Karkonosze National Park Entry ~€2 · Required for hiking trails; buy online to skip queues at the park entry points.
  • Wang Stave Church ~€3.50 · Tickets purchased on-site; interior access is restricted during Sunday services.
  • Where to eat
  • Sowiduch Browar i Restauracja — Regional Karkonosze dishes and craft beer brewed on-site; try the potato pancakes with goulash (~€15-22/pp).
  • Restauracja U Petera — Excellent fresh local trout and traditional Polish pierogi in a cozy, rustic mountain setting (~€12-18/pp).
  • In & around
  • Śnieżka Peak — 3-4 hour hike from Karpacz or take the cable car from Pec pod Sněžkou on the Czech side (45 min drive)
  • Szklarska Poręba & Kamieńczyk Waterfall — 30 min drive, featuring a dramatic 27-meter waterfall in a deep gorge
  • Chojnik Castle — 25 min drive, picturesque medieval ruins perched atop a high granite hill
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking near popular trailheads and the Wang Church fills up very early; carry cash (PLN) as some parking meters do not accept cards.
  • Weather on Śnieżka changes rapidly; even in summer, pack windproof layers, rain gear, and sturdy hiking boots.
  • Karpacz has very steep, narrow streets; ensure your vehicle's brakes are in good condition and be prepared for tight parallel parking.
  • Interesting facts
  • The Wang Stave Church was originally built in Vang, Norway, in the 12th century and was meticulously moved to Karpacz in 1842.
  • Karpacz features a famous gravitational anomaly spot on Strażacka Street where empty cars and liquid appear to roll uphill.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Bad Schandau

stop · DE

A historic spa town nestled along the Elbe River, serving as the ultimate gateway to the dramatic sandstone peaks, deep gorges, and lush hiking trails of Saxon Switzerland National Park.

Stay: €100-180/n town center or riverside — cheaper guesthouses in Krippen €60-100/n

Try: Sächsische Kartoffelsuppe (Saxon potato soup), local Elbe trout, Quarkkeulchen (sweet potato-quark pancakes)

  • Toskana Therme Bad Schandau ~€24-34 · Thermal bath entry is first-come, first-served; expect queues on rainy days.
  • Königstein Fortress ~€15 · Buy online to skip the main ticket queue during peak summer weekends.
  • Kirnitzschtalbahn Historic Tramway ~€9 day ticket · Purchase tickets directly from the conductor on board or at the main station machine.
  • Where to eat
  • Sendig-Restaurant — Upscale regional Saxon-Bohemian cuisine and fresh river fish served in an elegant riverside hotel — ~€45-70/pp
  • Gasthaus Zum Kirnitzschtal — Rustic tavern serving hearty local wild game, Saxon dumplings, and regional draft beers — ~€20-35/pp
  • Dampfschiff — Traditional German plates and fresh trout served on a scenic terrace overlooking the Elbe River — ~€25-40/pp
  • In & around
  • Bastei Bridge — 20 min drive, iconic sandstone bridge spanning deep gorges with dramatic panoramic views.
  • Schmilka Bio-Village — 10 min drive, a charming eco-village featuring a historic working watermill, organic bakery, and brewery.
  • Kuhstall — 15 min drive + 30 min hike, a massive natural sandstone arch with a steep staircase leading to a high viewpoint.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in the town center and near major trailheads is strictly regulated and fills up very early; utilize public transit or hotel parking.
  • Many mountain huts, rustic restaurants, and local transport options in the national park are strictly cash-only.
  • To avoid heavy crowds on the popular Bastei Bridge and Schrammsteine trails, aim to start your hikes before 8:30 AM.
  • Interesting facts
  • Bad Schandau is the oldest spa town in Saxon Switzerland, officially recognized for its therapeutic iron-rich springs since 1809.
  • The historic Kirnitzschtalbahn tramway has been running through the valley since 1898 and is partially powered by solar energy.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Międzyzdroje

stop · PL

A lively Baltic resort nestled between the sandy beaches of the Pomeranian Bay and the dramatic, forested cliffs of Wolin National Park.

Stay: €70-130/n beachfront hotels — cheaper guesthouses €40-70/n

Try: Smażona ryba (fried Baltic cod or flounder), wędzona flądra (smoked flounder), gofry z bitą śmietaną (waffles with whipped cream)

  • Wolin National Park Bison Showcase ~€4 · Tickets purchased at the forest entrance; cash and card accepted.
  • Baltic Miniature Park ~€10 · Features models of Baltic lighthouses and miniature castles; open daily.
  • Where to eat
  • Kuter Smażalnia Ryb — Freshly caught fried cod and halibut served by weight, plus traditional fish soup (~€15-25/pp).
  • Restauracja Port — Elegant seafood dishes, Baltic herring tartare, and regional Polish cuisine near the pier (~€20-35/pp).
  • In & around
  • Kawcza Góra Viewpoint — 5 min drive or walk from town, offering dramatic Baltic cliff views via wooden stairs.
  • Świnoujście — 20 min drive, featuring historic Prussian forts and the tallest lighthouse on the Polish coast.
  • Turquoise Lake in Wapnica — 10 min drive, a stunning water-filled former chalk quarry surrounded by beech forests.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in the town center is strictly regulated with paid parking zones (SPP) active during the summer season.
  • The beach and pier get extremely crowded in July and August; walk east into the national park for wilder, quieter shores.
  • Mosquitoes can be relentless in the forested areas of Wolin National Park; bring strong repellent for hikes.
  • Interesting facts
  • Międzyzdroje hosts the annual Polish Festival of Stars, leaving behind a promenade lined with handprints of famous Polish actors.
  • The nearby cliffs of Wolin National Park are the highest in Poland, reaching up to 95 meters above the Baltic Sea.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Łeba

stop · PL

A coastal gateway famous for the shifting giant sand dunes of Słowiński National Park, offering a unique desert-meets-Baltic landscape with pristine beaches and pine forest trails.

Stay: €60-120/n near the beach — cheaper guesthouses (pokoje) €40-70/n

Try: Smażona flądra (fried flounder), wędzony dorsz (smoked cod), Baltic herring, sea buckthorn liqueur

  • Słowiński National Park Entry Ticket ~€2 · Mandatory for entering the dune paths; can be purchased online to skip queues at the Rąbka entrance.
  • Butterfly Museum Łeba ~€5 · A quirky indoor option in the town center featuring thousands of insect specimens.
  • Where to eat
  • Restauracja Kania — Highly rated local spot serving traditional Polish Baltic fish dishes like zander and cod, ~€15-25/pp.
  • Chata Rybacka — Rustic tavern specializing in regional fish soups and hearty smoked fish platters, ~€12-20/pp.
  • In & around
  • Stilo Lighthouse — 30 min drive, a historic steel lighthouse offering panoramic coastal views after a short forest hike.
  • Kluki Open-Air Museum — 50 min drive, preserved 19th-century Slovinian dwellings showcasing regional folk culture.
  • Sarbsko Lake — 10 min drive, a calmer freshwater lake ideal for windsurfing and quieter nature walks.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • The walk from the Rąbka park entrance to the moving dunes is about 5.5 km; rent a bicycle at the entrance or take the electric golf cart shuttle to save energy.
  • Parking near the national park entrance in Rąbka is paid and fills up very quickly during peak summer hours.
  • Mosquitoes and ticks can be highly active in the pine forests of the national park; bring strong insect repellent.
  • Interesting facts
  • The Lacka Dune (Góra Łącka) is constantly moving eastwards at a speed of 3 to 10 meters per year, slowly burying the surrounding pine forest.
  • During World War II, the remote dunes of Łeba were used by German forces as a secret testing ground for rocket weaponry, including the Rheintochter missile.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Hel

stop · PL

Perched at the tip of a wind-swept, 35-kilometer sandbar, Hel combines wild Baltic beaches and pine forest hikes with fascinating military bunkers and a historic fishing harbor.

Stay: €70-130/n near the harbor — cheaper private rooms (pokoje) €45-80/n

Try: Baltic herring (śledź) in oil, smoked flounder (flądra), Kashubian fish soup

  • Hel Seal Sanctuary (Fokarium) ~€3 · Pay at the turnstiles; try to time your visit with the feeding shows at 11:00 and 14:00.
  • Museum of Coastal Defence ~€5 · Tickets sold on-site; covers several scattered bunkers in the pine forest.
  • Hel Lighthouse ~€3 · Cash only at the entrance; requires climbing a narrow spiral staircase.
  • Where to eat
  • Maszoperia — Traditional Kashubian fish soup and baked halibut served in a cozy, museum-like historic fisherman's cottage (~€18-28/pp).
  • Restauracja Morgan — Freshly fried Baltic cod with cabbage salad and local craft beer in a maritime-themed setting (~€15-25/pp).
  • ToTu — Excellent herring appetizers and modern takes on local seafood dishes (~€16-26/pp).
  • In & around
  • Jurata — 15 min drive, an upscale resort town with a long wooden pier and quiet walking paths
  • Jastarnia — 20 min drive, featuring historic fisherman's huts, a harbor, and WWII bunker trails
  • Notes — read before you go
  • The single road (Route 216) leading down the peninsula gets severely gridlocked in July and August; consider taking the passenger ferry from Gdańsk if not driving.
  • Parking in Hel is strictly regulated and expensive; always confirm if your lodging offers a dedicated parking space.
  • The Baltic wind can be biting even in summer; pack a high-quality windbreaker for beach walks and forest hikes.
  • Interesting facts
  • The Hel Peninsula was a chain of isolated islands until ocean currents deposited enough sand to form a continuous strip of land in the 17th century.
  • Because of its strategic location, Hel was one of the very last pockets of Polish territory to surrender to Germany during the invasion of 1939.
  • The town's name has no linguistic connection to the English word 'hell'; it likely stems from an old Germanic word meaning 'heel' or 'dry land'.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details
